items.title: React UI & Component Architecture
paragraphs: WiseOSC builds maintainable React applications with clear component boundaries, composition patterns, and scalable folder structure. Whether you use JavaScript or TypeScript, WiseOSC emphasizes predictable props, accessible markup, and design tokens that align design and engineering.

From atomic design to feature-sliced or domain-driven folders, WiseOSC documents conventions so new engineers onboard quickly. Storybook or similar tooling can showcase components in isolation and catch regressions before they reach production.

items.title: Modern React: Hooks, State & Data Fetching
paragraphs: WiseOSC implements state with the right tool for the job: local state with useState/useReducer, server state with TanStack Query or SWR, and global UI state with Zustand, Redux Toolkit, or Context where appropriate—avoiding unnecessary re-renders and stale data.

Data fetching strategies include REST, GraphQL, and streaming patterns where frameworks support them. Error boundaries, suspense boundaries (where applicable), and retry policies keep UX resilient when networks fail.

items.title: React in Production: Next.js, Remix & SSR/SSG
paragraphs: When SEO, performance, and first-load time matter, WiseOSC delivers React on frameworks like Next.js or Remix with intentional rendering models: static generation, server rendering, or hybrid routes. WiseOSC aligns caching, revalidation, and edge delivery with your content and traffic patterns.
For apps that stay client-heavy, WiseOSC optimizes bundles with route-based code splitting, lazy loading, and tree-shaking-friendly imports so users download only what they need.

items.title: Performance, Accessibility & Core Web Vitals
paragraphs: React is fast when you measure: WiseOSC profiles renders, list virtualization, and expensive effects. Memoization is applied where it buys real wins—not everywhere—alongside image optimization and font strategies.

Accessibility is not optional: keyboard navigation, ARIA roles, focus management in dialogs, and color contrast are verified alongside automated and manual checks so your product works for every user and reduces compliance risk.

items.title: Testing & Quality for React Codebases
paragraphs: WiseOSC combines unit tests (Vitest/Jest), component tests (React Testing Library), and end-to-end tests (Playwright/Cypress) based on risk. Tests focus on behavior users care about—not implementation details that break on every refactor.
CI pipelines run linting, typecheck, and tests on every merge; visual regression can guard design systems when needed.

items.title: Migration, Legacy React & Long-Term Maintenance
paragraphs: Upgrading from class components, older React versions, or legacy bundlers is planned in phases: codemods, feature flags, and incremental routes reduce risk. WiseOSC documents breaking changes and validates performance after each step.
Ongoing maintenance covers dependency updates (React, router, major libs), security advisories, and roadmap alignment with React and framework release channels.

whyChoose.title: Why Choose WiseOSC for React Development

features.title: Sensible architecture, not hype
features.body: WiseOSC picks patterns that fit your team size and product stage—whether that is a lean SPA, a Next.js commerce site, or a large modular dashboard. You get clear boundaries, documentation, and handoff so React stays maintainable for years.

features.title: Flexible engagement
features.body: Staff augmentation, dedicated squad, or project-based delivery: WiseOSC embeds in your Slack/Jira/Git workflow and aligns on Definition of Done, release cadence, and on-call expectations for front-end changes.

features.title: Senior React engineers
features.body: WiseOSC's React developers ship production UIs daily: they know hooks pitfalls, concurrent features where relevant, and how to collaborate with design and API teams without slowing delivery.

keyThings.title: Key Things to Know About React

sections.heading: 1. Component model & reuse
sections.body: React encourages small, composable pieces of UI that are easier to test and evolve than monolithic templates. Teams share design systems and ship features faster when boundaries are clear.

sections.heading: 2. Huge ecosystem & hiring
sections.body: A large community means libraries, examples, and hiring pipelines for React skills. That ecosystem also requires discipline—choosing stable dependencies and upgrade paths matters.

sections.heading: 3. Works across stacks
sections.body: React powers marketing sites, SaaS dashboards, and hybrid mobile (via React Native). Skills transfer across projects when conventions and TypeScript usage are consistent.

sections.heading: Product interfaces
sections.body: Single-page apps, authenticated portals, admin tools, and content-heavy sites (often with Next.js) are common. The choice of SSR/SSG vs client-only depends on SEO and interactivity needs.

sections.heading: Design systems
sections.body: Organizations centralize buttons, forms, and patterns in a React design system consumed by multiple apps—speeding delivery and keeping UX consistent.

sections.heading: Concurrent features & roadmap
sections.body: React continues to evolve (e.g., concurrent rendering, transitions). Teams should plan upgrades and test on representative devices—not only the latest Chrome on fast networks.

sections.heading: Not a full framework alone
sections.body: React is a UI library; routing, data loading, and deployment are choices you layer on. WiseOSC helps you pick a coherent stack so you do not Frankenstein incompatible patterns.

items.question: Why is React popular for building web applications?
items.answer: React combines a strong component model with a massive ecosystem and excellent tooling. Teams can iterate quickly, share UI via design systems, and adopt frameworks like Next.js when SEO and performance require server rendering.

items.question: Should we use Next.js or plain React?
items.answer: Next.js (or similar) helps when you need routing, SSR/SSG, image optimization, and API routes out of the box. A Vite or CRA-style SPA can be enough for internal tools or highly dynamic apps where SEO is not a priority.

items.question: How do you handle performance in large React apps?
items.answer: WiseOSC measures first: bundle size, render counts, and Core Web Vitals. Then WiseOSC applies code splitting, memoization where justified, list virtualization, and asset optimization—always validated with profiling, not guesswork.

items.question: Is TypeScript necessary for React projects?
items.answer: Not mandatory, but TypeScript pays off as teams and codebases grow: fewer runtime surprises, safer refactors, and better editor support. WiseOSC can introduce it gradually on existing codebases.

items.question: What is your approach to testing React applications?
items.answer: WiseOSC prioritizes user-visible behavior with React Testing Library, supplement with unit tests for pure logic, and add E2E tests for critical flows. The mix depends on risk and release frequency.

items.question: Can you migrate our legacy React codebase?
items.answer: Yes. WiseOSC assesses version drift, dependency risk, and routing/state patterns, then proposes a phased migration—often alongside test coverage and feature flags—to reduce downtime and regressions.
